posts.name: Alexander Gillette
posts.cooked:
I’m very new to these wines but I think the prices will rise significantly in the near future. I’m betting big on it. When I decided I wanted to make them part of my cellar, I thought about how to go about acquiring them. In the last month, I’ve bought about 50 bottles. I’ve skipped the val vilaine and the creux d’enfer completely (for different reasons for each). I’ve bought just a bit of the ursules but not much really. My purchases have been mainly boloree, haut l’emblee and presle. The wines are not so easy to acquire obviously, and outside of recent vintages you are likely in the auction market if you want any particular wine in any quantity. This meant I had to buy before I tasted, which isn’t ideal. To help get to a point of certainty, I spoke to the best palates I know (including a few in the UK who were on these wines early and used to be able to acquire a reasonable percentage of the output). I decided that if I wanted them, it made sense to just buy whatever I could right now. I’ve only done that a few times in the past and I’ve yet to get it wrong, but we will see how I do this time.

Here is my thinking:
(1) The wines are obviously wonderful and made in very small quantities. Doesn’t take too many buyers trying to get a case before a 500-bottle production wine becomes very dear in the secondary market.
(2) The wines are singular and there isn’t any substitute. If you love them, there is really nothing to take their place if prices rise. So buyers are going to be less sensitive to increases.
(3) They aren’t yet that expensive relative to wines of similar quality. While $2-300 is a lot for a bottle of wine, for me these fall well above the average quality level at that price point.
(4) None of the people I spoke to think that the creux d’enfer, which is already very pricey, is Bouchard’s best wine. That would imply that the others have some room to run and catch up.
(5) In the early days I think these were viewed as a bit of an experiment and people wondered whether maybe they were best drunk young. While there have been some wines that caused concern, I don’t hear people questioning the age worthiness of the wines made 2012 and after.
(6) Selosse 2008 with a $400 wholesale price immediately disappearing at $3000 per bottle from retail shelves is an ominous harbinger of what can happen.

Alex

Generally agree with Alex’s post, but to put a finer point on some of the rationale:

(4) None of the people I spoke to think that the creux d’enfer, which is already very pricey, is Bouchard’s best wine. That would imply that the others have some room to run and catch up.

I think the price here just reflects the supply/demand curve. But I do think that Creux d’Enfer is clearly better than Prevost Fac-simile (and people pay $400 per for the latter).

(5) In the early days I think these were viewed as a bit of an experiment and people wondered whether maybe they were best drunk young. While there have been some wines that caused concern, I don’t hear people questioning the age worthiness of the wines made 2012 and after.

It’s not just 2012 and after–had a 2007 Bechalin a few months ago, and it was absolutely stunning. But the wines show significant vintage variation (which should be expected given his intent to make a real vintage wine and the small quantities), so I do think knowing the vintages and how they are expressed in his wines is important.

posts.name: Greg Kahn
posts.cooked:
I think the price here just reflects the supply/demand curve. But I do think that Creux d’Enfer is clearly better than Prevost Fac-simile (and people pay $400 per for the latter).

I think the Fac-similie is pretty vintage specific; the 13 (especially) and 14 are magic. The 15 and 16 are not. I’ve not had as much of Bouchard’s rose (not a surprise given the production size), but I have yet to have one that has hit the heights of Prevost’s best vintages. And I don’t say that as a slight to Bouchard’s wines in the slightest; but I think the Boloree is his best and most interesting wine.
I would also generally agree that the pricing on the Creux d’Enfre reflects scarcity more than anything else.

posts.name: Andrew Northup
posts.cooked:
I enjoy the wines young and old. Young for their incredible vibrancy and old for some of the texture and body they build, as well as other flavors that develop alongside the incredible fruit of their youth. A few quick tasting notes on slightly older to much older (by RdJ standards) releases over the last year, though not super detailed (and not providing much useful information at all really on the 11 Inflorescence other than that I enjoyed it):

  • 2009 Roses de Jeanne / Cédric Bouchard Champagne Blanc de Blancs La Haute-Lemblé - France, Champagne (12/24/2021)
    honey and ripe peaches on the nose. Creamy texture, nice weight, sweet red fruit, lychee, some apple pie on the back of the tongue. This is a crazy Blanc de Blancs.

Posted from CellarTracker

  • 2004 Roses de Jeanne / Cédric Bouchard Champagne Inflorescence Blanc de Noirs La Parcelle Côte de Béchalin - France, Champagne (8/26/2021)
    not really showing any age in the color. Nose shows a little honey, but otherwise sweet breads (not sweetbreads) and overripe pear nose, layers of flavor, with a little smokiness, a little vanilla, loads of orchard fruit and great acid on the finish. 30 minutes later all ruggedness has shaken off and it’s a stunner. The fruit and smokiness are there, the acid has dropped to an appropriate back level, the finish lasts for a minute. So incredible now, but zero rush on this one either.

Posted from CellarTracker

  • 2011 Roses de Jeanne / Cédric Bouchard Champagne Inflorescence Blanc de Noirs Côte de Val Vilaine - France, Champagne (3/27/2021)
    Let this bottle breathe and it turns into something truly magical. What a delicious wine, incredible fruits, slightest hint of aging. So good.

Posted from CellarTracker

I’m lucky to have back to 2002-2004, 2007 & 2008 Parcelle/Bechalin 2006 HL, 2007 Boloree, and 2008 Inflorescence/VV in my cellar, and I’m in no rush on any of them.

posts.name: Brad Baker
posts.cooked: The L 01 likely refers to classifying the printed label itself and has nothing to do with the wine in the bottle. When Cedric first started out, he left the information on disgorgement and vintage to the back label and to the importer or purchaser to put it on or request it. This led to a bit of a mess where you often had to trust information relayed when you purchased the wine or if the bottle had a disgorgement date, you could cross reference and make 95%+ educated guess. I don’t think there is any easy way to figure out the above Polaner Selections bottle without going back and trying to track down different Cedric Bouchard shipments of Polaner imports and see what changes may have occurred to the back label over the years. Even then, I am not sure you could narrow down the year exactly.

Justin,

If you actually have a Les Ursules that is a 2001 then you have a very, very rare find especially if you purchased it in the last decade. 2001 was only Cedric Bouchard’s second vintage and when this was released in 2003/2004, there really wasn’t much buzz going on yet. I don’t believe any came over to the US (as least not officially and not in any quantity). It wasn’t really until the 2002-2004 Les Ursules releases (which was an NV, but pure vintage wine at the time), that I recall recognition on Cedric Bouchard starting to slowly build and even then I never saw a bottle in the US.

posts.name: Justin Wittkopf
posts.cooked:
I did get a response from my email to the retailer with regard to my bottle. It’s very much like what you described, Brad. Additionally, he confirmed it was the Ursule’s vineyard.

Here is his response:

As for L01 Bouchard - oh boy! Haha I can barely read the labels of new release Bouchard. We had a massive shipment from Europe a few months back and it took the warehouse hours to try and read all the vintages.

This however I purchased years ago from a collector who was very early on Bouchard. Most his bottles came from Polaner. In the early days of Bouchard - Polaner didnt always have this information of the Vxx, especially as this was his second vintage and technically these wines aren’t declared as a vintage either.

L01 went only to Japan and Polaner - maybe someone else in the US, but I am not 100% sure. I know this becasue - ironically I poured this by the glass for a couple nights when I worked in Tokyo. The boxes of these had written vintages on them and it’s how we cross checked this and kept track.

It perhaps not as clear cut always with Bouchard as most champagne or honestly any small producer. We had a batch of Prevost that had different bottle codes than labels and I chose to just drink it because honestly I didn’t know what it was. But in this instance we declared it as such based off of past experiences and the vintages on the box.

posts.name: Brad Baker
posts.cooked:
Justin,

Cool response and it makes sense from my experience. I know the first two vintages of Bouchard went to Japan and a few places within Europe; Japan was actually the main market for Bouchard when he started. I didn’t think any went to Polaner until after the 2001 vintage, but I could be wrong. The writing on the box comment is also consistent. For Bouchard, I recall having to trust retailers back in the day or have them drag out the boxes to see what was written or labeled on them.

The response you have matches up to a possible 2001 Les Ursules and you just have to trust your source at this point. There may be little other way of knowing. If your bottles have an importer sticker on the back, that might give some clue, but even then not likely to be 100%.

The only other comment I will make is that if the L01 is on the front label like the picture above, it isn’t an indication of the vintage. It would be a coincidence that it happened to be a 2001.

posts.name: M. Ch@o
posts.cooked:
Is this just because I just wasn’t noticing or as people have mentioned, The producer came in vogue while case production are low?

The bolded is why. The largest production is VV with 4,000-5,000 bottles annually, with Ursules and Bechalin being the next largest. Presle, Boloree and Haute Lemble are all under 1,000 bottles annually, with Creux d’Enfer (rose) in the range of 200-300 annually. All you need are a few people for whom money is no object to really ramp up pricing. And for those who have not paid attention, Bechalin is no more in exchange for a larger parcel of Creux d’Enfer (which will be used to produce blanc de noir, not rose).

Also surprised you bought since I recalled you not really liking the ‘17 Ursules.

posts.name: Alexander Gillette
posts.cooked: I think there’s a lot of confusion about pricing because it isn’t very transparent. I’m not sure who is paying $175 for Val Vilaine. I assume the answer is “no one”. I think there are a lot more sellers out there that are looking to sell for top dollar. Lots of places that buy gray market and can afford to ask 2x the amount of retail and wait it out. The marginal cost of keeping the bottle is almost 0 so they can wait it out. Couple that with Collin creating the impression of scarcity and you have sellers who can ask for the moon. At the same time, there’s tons of Bouchard and Collin being sold at a reasonable markup. The real lesson here is to develop deep retail relationships and not hunt for the lowest price on each wine.

posts.name: Tyler Fabian
posts.cooked:
The Val Vilaine is certainly quite good, but I find Presle, Ursules, and Boloree a very significant step up… I don’t have enough experience with Haut Lemble, but IMHO VV is not worth $130-150 from a pure quality perspective.

I mostly agree, although given how champagne seems to categorically be going through the stratosphere, $130-150 seems like the new $90. With what the big houses are doing with DP, Krug, and Cristal, the Growers will only follow suit. The $70-90 look like an absolute steal, which is probably some of the reason bottles are being snatched up just as soon as they hit the market.

As a result, I’ve been loading up on all of my favorite QPR bottles like Le Mesnil, Vilmart GC, Laherte Brut Nature BdB, etc. I may also have bought a couple high end bottles before they become unobtanium.

For me personally, if VV goes above $150, I’m priced out. As a data point, Haut Lemble used to be $150. Funny what changes in 2 years.

posts.name: Doug Schulman
posts.cooked:
I have to say, I’ve had three bottles of Bouchard, and none have been that exciting. I assume they need age.

The style isn’t for everyone. I aged a few short term (maybe 4-5 years) and didn’t think improvement was happening or going to happen, but I could easily have been wrong. I often decanted the wines. I thought Val Vilaine was extremely well valued at the old pricing and would sometimes buy a bottle here and there of the other bottlings (never had the rosé, though), which I thought were priced about right. The Bolorée was very nice but too expensive for me, considering the quality. Anyway, even if I could get Val Vilaine for $90 now, I wouldn’t. There are too many other Champagnes I’d rather have in that price range. I would stock up again for more like $60, but those days are gone. Some people on this thread are probably glad there are people like me who don’t like the wines as much as they do.

posts.name: Tyler Fabian
posts.cooked: In my experience with CB, aging helps, but decanting is far more beneficial. Give them a minimum of 90 mins in a decanter and they’ll certainly improve.

posts.name: Alexander Gillette
posts.cooked: Interesting for me to hear people say that they greatly prefer other champagne at the same price. For me, my success rate on champagne is that I find about 1 out of every 20 bottles enjoyable. The ones that I do enjoy tend to have a price tag roughly in the same range as Roses de Jeanne. For Bouchard, with the exception of some screwy ‘09s, my hit rate is pretty perfect. I think there are pretty good and obvious reasons why someone isn’t doesn’t generally into champagne would find these compelling, and someone who loves champagne would find these puzzling. I wish this was one of those cases where my idiosyncratic preferences geared me towards something that was less expensive and easier to find, but so far I’ve yet to find a substitute for these and I’m happy to enough to be the market-clearing buyer at the prices I’m seeing.

Funny you should I ask. I picked up the ‘08 du levant this week. I also had the longitude or latitude at Ross’s suggestion - can’t remember which. I wouldn’t purchase either again and didn’t see anything stylistically unique in them. I like Collin - the Chardonnay-based wines taste like white burgundy. If you’d served me one in 2001, I would have likely passed it off as roughly the same as a very, very good meursault. In 2022, where ver, very good meursault tastes like someone fired a Remington 870 next to your head, and then devolves into vinegar before it’s ready to open, I think they are very much worth owning. I wouldn’t chase them, but if they pop up I will buy them and they’ve popped up a lot recently. The maillons is very fruity and delicious. The rose is like a middling red burgundy. Cristal with about 20 years of age on it is really beautiful for me, but that’s obviously a significant cash commitment if you haven’t owned the bottles since release. I enjoyed the Egly Ouriet crayeres (although those with more than a few years post release have been less to me liking) but not the other Egly wines I’ve tried. I’ve been around the horn pretty good. Been doing this for 20 years. Got a cellar full of burgundy and German Riesling. I would count Bouchard as unique.

posts.name: Justin Wittkopf
posts.cooked:
Like several of the other posters in this thread, I have been buying Bouchard fairly fervently over the last 3 years after having a few bottles that really wowed me. Before the holidays I bought a case of the 2019 VV at “normal” prices and drank through most of them before seeing the huge price hikes. Wish I hadn’t gone through them so quickly, but I felt they were the best VV’s in the last several years.

Yesterday I opened a 2009 Boloree with some friends. I hadn’t had a Boloree from this vintage so we as excited to try it. I have to admit I was a tad disappointed in it. Maybe my expectations were too high? Initially, this was very tart, with overwhelming orchard fruit notes. I was missing the delicate and nuanced fruit tones I usually get with Bouchard wines. The perlage was a little more harsh than previous Bouchard bottles. Now this did become more finessed with time, so maybe it was coming into its own with more time open. I know some have talked about decanting these wines, but I didn’t think it necessary on a 13 year old bottle. To be honest, I preferred the 2019 VV we had the weak prior. I have few more of these so I will see how the evolve over time.

posts.name: Alexander Gillette
posts.cooked:
I don’t think anyone would take issue with Collin trying to have his wines in restaurants. But requiring US distribution to only sell to restaurants, and punishing retailers who put the wines on wine searcher, is extreme. It’s a little bit disingenuous to suggest that this is a good thing for consumers. It’s not. It limits access to wines to very expensive restaurant experiences. There is no doubt that it increases the price of consuming the wine. It reduces everyone’s ability to buy the wines, age them, and drink them in a setting of their choosing where you don’t have to worry about the table being turned in 90 minutes, whether the wine needs to open up, and lots of other things that people on this board care about. And it means that those people who have supported Collin for years and helped get the wines recognition (I’m not included in this group - and I’m not talking about people who have been buying for 3 or 4 years - but people who have really been buying for a while) are cut out of the loop and can’t get the wines they’ve bought for years. That’s not really the way it’s supposed to work. It also means those that buy at the cellar door and flip, and those that otherwise buy outside of official channels, can really increase prices because of the greatly reduced supply. The least price sensitive buyer sets the market price. That’s what’s happening now. Does anyone think that’s a good thing that they’d like to see replicated across other producers? If so, then I wouldn’t think you’d need to cellar anything. You could just save your dollars for restaurant purchases.

I’m a diehard capitalist, but this strikes me as a terrible harbinger for the wine world. I don’t really understand the desire to control the distribution of a bottle of wine to such a great degree and notion that he wants his wines in restaurants strikes me as an answer rather than an explanation. Is it about the status of having the wines in the world’s great restaurants? Has he considered some sort of exclusive deal with Outback Steakhouse where he could put the entire production of Maillons in their chains? That would do a really great job of letting people get access to his wines in restaurants - even better than he’s doing now.

If Olivier’s real goal is what William says it is, I think he can do a much better job of putting his wines in restaurants at reasonable prices without creating all of the other knock on effects that people are lamenting. I hope other producers don’t take this approach. It would be unfortunate.

Alex

This reminds me of the discussion people have about the “true” price of allocated Burgundy. You are now effectively arguing that the secondary market price of Collin is its “true” market price. Which is fine, but then it’s no different than most any other allocated wine and I wish people just admitted that the reason for the different treatment is that it was cheaper recently.

I will also add, again, two points:

  1. For the quality of the wine, the price increase on restaurant lists has been perfectly reasonable.
  2. There have been a number of wines that have precipitously escalated in value recently despite no similar restrictions on sale - Bouchard and Cappellano are easy examples, a few Burgundy producers. Whenever a producer catches fire, the secondary market pricing above the allocation price always rises very quickly.

I understand that. I just don’t think you’re correct because these wines are not subject to elastic price curves - Bouchard is cheaper on wine lists (I do see them) than at secondary retail pricing.

Or, to put it this way - if you doubled the amount of CLB available to retail in NYC, would that meaningfully lower its price in the secondary market? I think it would have almost no effect, since it would still be sold through allocation channels. That’s basically what happening with Collin, Bouchard, Lamy Caillat, etc. They key is massive demand, not the quantity constraint.

I’ve made the point before, but there used to be a reasonable amount of Enfers available at retail on Wine Searcher which disappeared the day it got 100 points*. I also know people who had no interest in me bringing bottles of Roises to dinners last year who now post unicorn emojis after buying it off wine lists. It really is about inflection points for these types of wines more than it is about supply and demand.

*I’m not saying that was the only driver, but it had a lot to do with it.

posts.name: Jayson Cohen
posts.cooked:
I love his wines, and I don’t doubt his motivations. But I question the economics/financial model and incentives it creates if the overall goal was to decrease consumer pricing and broaden access. No doubt multiple intersecting factors are at play. But once the dominant channels in the US become privileged sales to select buyers behind the scenes and restaurants with their typical wine pricing programs, when coupled with quality-driven demand, is what has happened so surprising? It was predictable.

But to one of Greg’s point I think, I’m not sure the Wine Searcher ban did much. Maybe at the margins it did. It made it an imperative to get on the privileged list or otherwise pay restaurant pricing at restaurants or in secondary market.

The market will take care of and eliminate good restaurant deals except in very rare cases.

It’s not very different in effect than what’s happened with high end Burgundy.

posts.name: Alexander Gillette
posts.cooked:
Just had a discussion with a few friends off line about this thread. After having a good laugh about a post or two, here was a buddy’s response: “I’ve been buying Collin longer than pretty much anyone in the United States. Maybe Peter Liem bought them earlier. That I have to beg for a single bottle from 10 different retailers is fuck’d up. And that this is by design of the winemaker, who wants these people to get less access to the wine, bothers me.”

I think that is a reasonable response. Life is full of bummers, and you will encounter things that you find annoying, and people with bad manners, both within and without wine boards and wine-buying and you can’t focus on all of them. I don’t really buy Collin so I don’t have a dog in this hunt, but I can see the argument that it feels fucked up in way that is worth marking - like when we see a white rhino. That’s my analysis of Collin’s new strategy - fuck’d up. I hope it doesn’t become a trend. I’m glad that no one else does this.
